.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:521:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer using the web UI on a real-world-sized workspace (tens of projects × hundreds of beads each), the beads list opens in under a second and the cross-project all-beads view stays usable. Before any perf fix lands, we can reproduce the problem and measure improvement — not guess.\n\n**Acceptance criteria:**\n\n1. **Perf harness exists and is reusable.**\n   - Fixture builder generates `N × M` beads across projects with configurable shape (status mix, label density, dep count). Reuses the real `bead.Store` write path (no fake writer).\n   - Benchmark covers at minimum: `bead(id:)`, `beadsByProject`, `beads` (cross-project), `docGraph`, `sessions`. Adding a new target is a single-table-row change.\n   - Each target produces p50/p95/p99 for in-process calls and end-to-end HTTP. Results write to a human-readable report (markdown or JSON) plus a machine-readable artifact suitable for CI diffing later.\n\n2. **Baseline recorded.** First run of the harness on the current `main` binary is stored in the bead notes and in `docs/helix/04-observe/perf/YYYY-MM-DD-baseline.md` (or equivalent existing location). Numbers include hardware + go version footer.\n\n3. **BeadsByProject scope fix.** `BeadsByProject` calls a new `GetBeadSnapshotsForProject(projectID, …)` that opens exactly one project's store. Unit test asserts only that project's store is opened (no iteration over other projects).\n\n4. **Filter pushdown.** Status/label/projectID filters apply at the per-file / per-entry parse boundary, not post-materialization. Benchmark captures before/after for the common filtered case (e.g., `status=open`).\n\n5. **Perf targets on fixture (10 projects × 500 beads = 5000 beads total):**\n   - `bead(id:)` p95 ≤ 50ms in-process / ≤ 200ms HTTP (already covered by `ddx-ad0db8fd`; this bead may inherit the work).\n   - `beadsByProject(first=50)` p95 ≤ 75ms in-process / ≤ 250ms HTTP.\n   - `beads(first=50, no projectID)` p95 ≤ 200ms in-process / ≤ 500ms HTTP.\n   These are targets for the **first** pass (scope + pushdown). If they are not met with (1)+(2), escalate to the in-memory index (step 3 of Part 2 in the description) and update these targets with the new baseline.\n\n6. **Stale-read safety.** Regardless of fix chosen, a test writes a bead via `ddx bead update`, then queries it via GraphQL, then asserts the new value is visible on the very next request. Works under concurrency: N concurrent readers + writers interleaved without torn reads or panics.\n\n7. **CI posture (soft).** The harness is runnable via `make bench-graphql` (or the project's equivalent). CI wiring to fail on \u003e25% regression is a follow-up bead — recorded in this bead's notes with the proposed threshold but not implemented here.\n\n8. **Playwright smoke.** Opening `/beads` on the fixture project returns an interactive (clickable) list within 1s. Opening the cross-project `/beads` page returns within 2s. These are ceilings, not p95 targets — guards against outright regression, not fine-grained perf.\n\n9. **Cross-reference.** This bead's notes list `ddx-ad0db8fd` (bead detail) and `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions shard/index) as the other two bead/session perf threads that should inherit this harness.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"7a2078a59d9885cb94f4c188ea95ce5149f969cd","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:41:52.271330549Z","description":"## Observed\n\nPer-project `/beads` page is noticeably slow; the cross-project \"all beads\" view is substantially slower. Related: `ddx-ad0db8fd` already identified the single-bead-detail slowness caused by the same storage pattern.\n\n## Root cause (same pattern, different call site)\n\n`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_beads.go:22,40` —\n\n- `Beads` (cross-project) and `BeadsByProject` (single-project) both call `r.State.GetBeadSnapshots(status, label, projectID, search)`.\n- `GetBeadSnapshots` (`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:49-105`) iterates every registered project, constructs a `bead.Store` per project, calls `ReadAll()` (scan + JSONL parse of every bead file), filters in Go. Cost is `O(projects × beads_per_project)` on every call, independent of the filter/page size.\n- All filtering (status, label, project, search) is Go-side, post-read. The page-size window is applied in `beadConnectionFromSnapshots` after the full corpus is already materialized.\n- Cross-project \"all beads\" = same loop, just without the projectID filter. It is the worst case by design.\n\nThis is the same shape that's breaking the Sessions feed (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) and single-bead detail (`ddx-ad0db8fd`). A point fix here is insufficient — we need a measurement surface first so we stop relying on anecdote.\n\n## Proposed direction\n\n### Part 1 — Perf harness (foundational; enables everything else)\n\nIntroduce a reusable GraphQL perf harness under `cli/internal/server/perf/` or `cli/internal/server/graphql/bench_test.go`:\n\n- Seeded fixture builder: generates N projects × M beads with configurable distribution of status/labels/dependencies. Reuses existing store primitives (no fake data layer).\n- Go benchmark + scriptable HTTP runner (wrapper around an already-running server) reporting p50/p95/p99 per query shape.\n- Targets covered on day one: `bead(id:)`, `beadsByProject(projectID, first, after, status, label, search)`, `beads(projectID=nil, first, after, status, label)`, `docGraph`, `sessions` (so the sessions shard work `ddx-2ceb02fa` inherits this harness). New query targets are added by writing a single table row.\n- Thresholds are **recorded, not asserted**, in the first pass. CI surfaces a regression if a threshold is breached beyond a configured delta (e.g., +25% p95). Initial pass is \"land the harness and record baseline\"; a follow-up wires it into CI.\n\n### Part 2 — Fix the scan pattern\n\nMinimum viable, in priority order:\n\n1. **Scope by project when the query is scoped.** `BeadsByProject` should call a new `GetBeadSnapshotsForProject(projectID, filters…)` that only opens that one project's store. Trivial change; eliminates the cross-project factor for the common case.\n2. **Early filter pushdown.** `GetBeadSnapshots` should apply status/label/projectID filters at the file-parse boundary where cheap, not after materializing every bead. (Still reads every file — deeper fix below.)\n3. **Lightweight in-memory index with invalidation.** After (1) and (2), if \"all beads\" or search is still slow, introduce an index keyed `{projectID, beadID}` with basic metadata (status, labels, title, updatedAt). Refresh on bead write + debounced filesystem watch. Full-body reads stay lazy — the index never holds bodies. This is the same storage discipline we adopted for sessions in `ddx-2ceb02fa`.\n\nThe harness from Part 1 is the gate for choosing between (2) and (3); don't commit to the index without numbers.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Full-text search infrastructure (tracked separately if needed).\n- Changing the on-disk bead format.\n- Cross-node federation performance. .ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:535:{"acceptance":"- [ ] execute-loop runs post-merge review by default\n- [ ] Review tier auto-selected (max(impl_tier+1, smart))\n- [ ] Review verdict JSON is stable and documented\n- [ ] APPROVE closes bead with review evidence attached\n- [ ] REQUEST_CHANGES reopens bead via ddx bead reopen and triggers next tier\n- [ ] BLOCK stops escalation and flags for human\n- [ ] --no-review opt-out works; 'review:skip' label works\n- [ ] Integration test: impossible-AC bead produces REQUEST_CHANGES forever without infinite-looping (bounded by max-tier)","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-15T05:36:55Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"3766264","closing_commit_sha":"3c149a123610788c014051e0bae082cb89aee69d","created_at":"2026-04-15T00:49:48.524036278Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-a317208c","depends_on_id":"ddx-fdf031c9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-15T00:49:53Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-a317208c","depends_on_id":"ddx-518b2690","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-15T00:49:53Z"}],"description":"execute-loop currently closes a bead on merge. There is no automated post-merge review step validating the commit against the bead's AC. Real-world practice requires a second-opinion agent (typically a higher-capability tier) to produce APPROVE / REQUEST_CHANGES.\n\n## Principle\n\nReview is on-by-default with zero flags. The tool picks a tier \u003e= the implementation tier (defaulting to smart). The user overrides only in exceptional cases.\n\n## Default flow\n\n- Implementation agent runs at current tier (see ddx-dc231144 tier-based escalation)\n- On successful merge, tool automatically renders a review prompt via 'ddx bead review' (ddx-fdf031c9)\n- Review agent runs at max(impl_tier + 1, smart)\n- Review result is structured JSON: { verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK, findings: [...] }\n- APPROVE → close bead, attach review evidence\n- REQUEST_CHANGES → reopen bead via 'ddx bead reopen' (ddx-44084b3e), append review body, let the tier-escalation policy pick the next attempt\n- BLOCK → stop escalating, flag for human\n\n## Rare overrides\n\n- --no-review (skip review entirely — e.g., doc-only beads in a tight loop)\n- --review-harness / --review-model (explicit reviewer override for debug)\n- Per-bead opt-out via a 'review:skip' label on the bead\n\n## Dependencies\n\n- ddx-fdf031c9 (ddx bead review command) provides the review prompt template\n- ddx-44084b3e (ddx bead reopen) provides the atomic reopen primitive\n- ddx-518b2690 (plain --output text) makes extracting the review verdict trivial\n- ddx-5bac3c6b (provider health probe) feeds the reviewer tier selection\n\n## Context\n\nObserved during axon ADR-018 execute-loop:\n- qwen3-coder-next merged A1 with real AC gaps (missing round-trip tests, zero Postgres coverage, scope creep)\n- Without review, the bead would have closed silently and the gaps would surface only at epic rollup — much later and much more expensive to fix\n- Review took 41s + a few minutes of reading. Cheap relative to the real cost of shipping broken code downstream.","events_attachment":"ddx-a317208c/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-15T05:57:25.094183105Z","id":"ddx-a317208c","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","area:agent","area:exec","kind:feature","gap:review-loop"],"notes":"\u003coperator-feedback 2026-04-15\u003e\nCritical gap discovered during axon ADR-018 execute-loop:\n\nWhen a bead is reopened after a REQUEST_CHANGES review, the REVIEW FINDINGS must be surfaced to the next execute-bead attempt. Today this fails silently because:\n\n1. Review findings posted via 'ddx bead update --notes' are NOT included in the execute-bead prompt.md. Execute-bead threads only \u003cdescription\u003e and \u003cacceptance\u003e into the XML payload.\n\n2. Therefore a reopen-via-notes does NOT communicate the review findings to the next agent. The agent reads the original description + AC, sees the existing code already matches the description, and declares 'already_satisfied' — auto-closing the bead.\n\n3. The workaround I had to use was updating the DESCRIPTION directly with the review findings. But then the description is no longer a stable spec — it's a moving target carrying review history.\n\n## Required behavior\n\nWhen a bead is reopened with a review verdict attached, execute-bead's generated prompt MUST include the most recent REQUEST_CHANGES review findings as a distinct section, e.g.:\n\n  \u003creview-findings iteration=\"2\" reviewer=\"claude-opus-4-6\"\u003e\n  BLOCKING: UserIdentity field 'subject' should be 'external_id' per ADR-018 §3\n  PARTIAL: aud-array test asserts serde_json::Error instead of AuthError::CredentialMalformed\n  ...\n  \u003c/review-findings\u003e\n\nAnd the execute-bead instructions should read: 'If \u003creview-findings\u003e is present, address each BLOCKING finding before claiming the work complete. Do not declare already_satisfied if any BLOCKING finding is unaddressed.'\n\n## Observed failure mode\n\nSonnet at iter 2 of axon-43ed769b read the prompt (which had the original description with 'subject' and the unchanged AC), verified all type shapes match, ran tests green, and wrote a no_changes_rationale declaring the work complete. The 'already_satisfied' close path auto-closed the bead. The actual BLOCKING issue (wrong field name) was invisible to the execute-bead layer. I had to manually reopen and rewrite the description with fix-up instructions inline.\n\n## This should be part of the post-review epic scope\n\nMaking review findings round-trip through the prompt is half of what 'post-review agent in execute-loop' means. Adding to this bead's scope:\n\n- Review verdict JSON includes a 'findings' array with severity (blocking|partial|nit) and location\n- Reopen path persists the findings to the bead\n- execute-bead prompt rendering pulls latest review findings and injects them as a distinct XML section\n- Execute-bead instructions teach the agent to treat blocking findings as first-class work items\n- no_changes / already_satisfied paths check for unaddressed blocking findings before closing\n\u003c/operator-feedback\u003e","owner":"ddx","parent":"ddx-32b3008e","priority":1,"session_id":"eb-451c8bc5","status":"closed","title":"exec: post-merge review agent on-by-default in execute-loop","updated_at":"2026-04-15T05:57:50.41702859Z"}

.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:580:{"acceptance":"- [ ] docs/EXECUTE-LOOP.md exists and covers all four sections above (topology, invariants, runbook, troubleshooting)\n- [ ] README links to the execute-loop doc from the agent command section\n- [ ] Runbook includes explicit recovery steps for the 'both sides diverged' scenario that axon is currently in\n- [ ] The doc is reviewed by someone who did NOT write it before landing\n- [ ] A new operator can follow the runbook to add a secondary machine without assistance\n- [ ] Existing patterns (checkpoint commits, ort merges) are documented as legacy and expected to disappear post-P0-beads","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","created_at":"2026-04-14T12:21:36.832469975Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-b0374901","depends_on_id":"ddx-bd42f640","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-14T12:21:51Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-b0374901","depends_on_id":"ddx-83ec1430","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-14T12:21:51Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-b0374901","depends_on_id":"ddx-6aa50e57","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-14T12:21:51Z"}],"description":"Sixth and final child of ddx-b7ee0d6f. The axon divergence happened partly because the multi-machine topology was never written down. Two machines (eitri and sindri) both ran execute-loop against the same project, neither was designated authoritative, and nobody had defined the pull/push contract. The result was predictable once you look at it, but there was no document that said 'this is wrong' before it happened.\n\n## Required documentation\n\nWrite docs/EXECUTE-LOOP.md (or expand the ddx README) covering:\n\n### Topology models\n\n**Single-machine solo**: one machine, one execute-loop. No coordination needed beyond the fetch/push/rebase triad (ddx-bd42f640, ddx-83ec1430, ddx-6aa50e57).\n\n**Primary + secondary(s)**: one machine is authoritative (pushes to origin). Secondaries pull-only — they can run the execute-loop if and only if the fetch bead guarantees they fast-forward from origin before every bead, and they refuse to land any work locally. Useful for running review/eval passes in parallel without forking.\n\n**Peer mesh**: multiple machines all push to origin. Each must implement the fetch → verify ancestry → rebase → push invariant from the P0 beads. Requires the tracker concurrency bead (ddx-2791dc4f) to be solved or all machines will step on each other's beads.jsonl.\n\n### Invariants\n\n- Before claiming a bead: local HEAD must be a strict ancestor of origin/\u003cdefault-branch\u003e (or equal). If not, refuse to claim.\n- After landing a bead successfully: push --ff-only or mark land_conflict and stop. Never leave work trapped locally.\n- Between sibling workers on the same machine: rebase onto the latest master, never merge.\n- Every bead claim must be visible to every other execute-loop before the claimer starts work (tracker concurrency).\n\n### Operator runbook\n\n- How to add a new worker machine safely (fresh clone + verify divergence is 0 + start loop)\n- How to recover from a diverged state (the current axon situation): reconcile manually, reset secondaries to origin, restart loops\n- How to migrate existing diverged projects to the new safety model (one-time cleanup)\n- How to stop all execute-loops globally (e.g., during a freeze window)\n\n### Troubleshooting\n\n- 'divergence_detected' event: what caused it and how to recover\n- 'push_rejected' event: what caused it and how to recover\n- 'checkpoint before merge' commits in history: legacy pattern, should not appear after ddx-6aa50e57 lands\n- Multiple 'Merge made by ort' commits: same as above, legacy pattern\n\n### ADR reference\n\nOnce the tracker concurrency bead (ddx-2791dc4f) picks option A / B / C, cite the ADR here so operators understand the coordination model.\n\n## Why this matters\n\nThe P0 beads will prevent future divergence, but operators need to understand WHY the rules exist to avoid bypassing them with --force or manual git operations. Documentation is what keeps the fixes effective a year from now.","id":"ddx-b0374901","issue_type":"task","labels":["docs","execute-loop","orchestration"],"parent":"ddx-b7ee0d6f","priority":2,"session_id":"eb-ee39eb4","status":"closed","title":"execute-loop: document multi-machine topology and operator contract","updated_at":"2026-04-14T15:45:17.048232166Z"}

.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:612:{"acceptance":"1. `ddx work --once --local` succeeds (exits 0 with at least one successful bead close OR a clean 'no ready work' exit) on a project that has only `.ddx/beads.jsonl` and no `.ddx/config.yaml`, when the global agent config has at least one provider configured.\n\n2. `ddx work` does not emit `routing under-specified` for any bead unless the global agent config is itself empty — that case must produce a clear error pointing at the global config, not a per-project routing flag list.\n\n3. Triage complexity gate is enabled by default. The warning 'triage complexity gate is disabled; coarse beads may waste dispatch attempts' must not appear in the default invocation. Opt-out flag exists and is documented.\n\n4. Auto-tier selection picks a tier from bead metadata alone (labels, kind, estimated scope) for beads that have those fields, with the global default_provider for routing when no per-project config is present.\n\n5. Coarse-bead handling: when no tier can confidently execute a bead (e.g. it spans more than N files or N hours of estimated work), DDx either decomposes the bead (records this as decomposition events on the bead) or emits a `needs_triage` status (not `execution_failed`) with a clear reason.\n\n6. Failure recovery: transient failures (network, rate limit, timeout) retry within the selected tier; substantive failures (probe_ok+exec_fail or harness errors) escalate to the next tier per existing escalation logic.\n\n7. Integration test in the DDx test suite: `tests/integration/zero-config-work_test.go` (or equivalent) creates a temp project with one bead and a minimal global config, runs `ddx work --once --local`, asserts the bead closes successfully with no project config files created.\n\n8. Documentation update: `docs/getting-started.md` (or equivalent) shows the zero-config flow as the default, with project config presented as an advanced override rather than a prerequisite.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"e2f720e85b198785adf09c7e699b7921683bea1b","created_at":"2026-05-01T20:13:29.172609315Z","description":"Operators should not need to author any project-level routing config (.ddx/config.yaml, --harness, --profile, --model, --provider) for `ddx work` to drain a project's bead queue. The platform should auto-select harness, model, and provider based on bead complexity, retry/fall back on failures, and decompose work that is too coarse for one dispatch.\n\n## Observed behavior (HELIX project, 2026-05-01)\n\nRunning `ddx work --once --local` from `/Users/erik/Projects/helix`:\n\n```\nwarning: triage complexity gate is disabled; coarse beads may waste dispatch attempts\n▶ helix-13cfe23f: Align HELIX execution contract to shipped DDx execute-loop / execute-bead capabilities\n✓ helix-13cfe23f → error: routing under-specified: pass at least one of Harness/Profile/ModelRef/Model/Provider, or auto-selection inputs\nproject: /Users/erik/Projects/helix\ncompleted: 1  |  successes: 0  |  failures: 1\n```\n\nWorkarounds tried, all failed or were rejected by users as the wrong layer to address this:\n\n1. `ddx work --once --local --profile cheap` — same routing-under-specified error.\n2. `ddx work --once --local --harness openrouter` — `harness \"openrouter\": no configured provider matches type \"openrouter\" (available: [])`, despite `~/.config/agent/config.yaml` having multiple openrouter-typed providers.\n3. `--min-tier cheap` — unknown flag.\n\nBy contrast, the same `ddx work` invocation succeeds for the DDx project itself — workers in `ddx server workers list` show recent successful runs against ddx-* beads. So the per-project config gap is the difference, not anything global.\n\n## Expected behavior\n\n`ddx work` (and `ddx agent execute-loop` directly) should:\n\n1. Auto-select routing for each bead with no flags and no project config required, given that the global agent config (`~/.config/agent/config.yaml`) has providers configured.\n2. Make routing decisions from bead complexity: doc-only or single-file changes → cheap tier; multi-file refactors → standard; cross-cutting work → smart. The 'triage complexity gate' warning suggests this exists but is disabled by default.\n3. Retry on transient failure within the same tier; escalate to a higher tier on substantive failure (this is documented behavior — `winning_tier=exhausted` events show the escalation chain works once dispatched, but auto-routing has to dispatch first).\n4. When a bead is too coarse for any tier to execute reliably, either decompose it into child beads or surface a 'needs operator triage' status with a clear reason — never silently wedge with `routing under-specified`.\n5. Default to using the global agent config's `default_provider` when no project override exists, instead of treating the absence of project config as a hard error.\n\n## Why this matters\n\n- **Adoption friction**: Every new project that adopts DDx hits this wall. Authoring `.ddx/config.yaml` requires understanding DDx's routing internals before an operator can run `ddx work` once.\n- **Methodology coupling**: HELIX's value proposition includes 'supervised autopilot' — the autopilot is supposed to drain ready beads. When `ddx work` requires per-project routing config, HELIX projects have to ship operational setup that distracts from the methodology.\n- **Operator surprise**: The DDx-vs-HELIX boundary contract (HELIX/CONTRACT-001) explicitly says HELIX should not own routing config. But projects can't run `ddx work` without it. That's a contract violation manifesting as setup burden.\n- **Triage gate disabled by default**: The warning 'triage complexity gate is disabled; coarse beads may waste dispatch attempts' is emitted on every run but the operator has no obvious way to enable it. The default should be enabled with a documented opt-out, not the inverse.\n\n## Suggested approach (not prescriptive)\n\n1. Audit the auto-selection input requirements: what inputs are needed to pick a tier when no project config is present? Likely the bead's labels, `kind`, file-touch estimate, and prior-attempt history. All available without operator input.\n2. Make 'use global agent config defaults' the fallback when project routing is unspecified — currently the code requires explicit project routing OR explicit flags.\n3. Enable triage complexity gate by default; document the opt-out flag.\n4. Add an integration test: `ddx work --once --local` succeeds against a fresh project with no `.ddx/config.yaml` and a single bead in `.ddx/beads.jsonl`, given a minimal `~/.config/agent/config.yaml` with one provider.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Changing the global agent config schema. The fix is to reduce dependency on per-project config, not redesign the global one.\n- Per-bead model overrides for advanced users — those should remain available via flags but should not be required for the common case.\n\n## Reference\n\n- HELIX alignment review where this surfaced: `docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-05-01-public-examples.md` (in the easel/helix repo). The audit's 12 follow-up beads were drained via direct sub-agent dispatch instead of `ddx work` because of this gap.","events_attachment":"ddx-b790449b/events.jsonl","execute-loop-no-changes-count":2,"id":"ddx-b790449b","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","area:routing","area:cli","kind:enhancement","priority:high"],"priority":1,"session_id":"eb-1d66753f","status":"closed","title":"ddx work should auto-route execution without project config","updated_at":"2026-05-02T01:42:42.099379643Z"}
.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:615:{"acceptance":"- [ ] All six child beads are filed and actionable\n- [ ] The first three (fetch, push, rebase) ship as a coordinated release since none of them are useful without the others\n- [ ] A test harness exists that simulates two parallel workers on the same machine and asserts no merge commits are produced\n- [ ] A test harness exists that simulates two machines with divergent histories and asserts the second machine's claim is refused until reconciliation\n- [ ] docs/EXECUTE-LOOP.md (or equivalent) documents the fetch/rebase/push invariant and the multi-machine topology\n- [ ] The epic closes when all children close AND an end-to-end test has run the loop for 10 iterations across two simulated machines without divergence","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","created_at":"2026-04-14T12:18:37.269117551Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-b7ee0d6f","depends_on_id":"ddx-bd42f640","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-14T12:21:51Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-b7ee0d6f","depends_on_id":"ddx-83ec1430","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-14T12:21:51Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-b7ee0d6f","depends_on_id":"ddx-6aa50e57","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-14T12:21:51Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-b7ee0d6f","depends_on_id":"ddx-2791dc4f","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-14T12:21:51Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-b7ee0d6f","depends_on_id":"ddx-42dd982c","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-14T12:21:51Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-b7ee0d6f","depends_on_id":"ddx-b0374901","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-14T12:21:51Z"}],"description":"Root cause epic for the multi-machine divergence problem discovered on axon 2026-04-14 during a planning session. Execute-loop treats master as a private branch and produces divergent histories that never reconcile with origin.\n\n## Evidence\n\nOn axon, two machines running execute-loop (`eitri` and `sindri`) produced 14 days of independent history from a shared merge-base at 27c54ded (2026-04-06):\n\n- eitri: 134 local commits that never reached origin\n- sindri: 336 commits that pushed to origin\n- Same FEAT-026 slot used for two unrelated features (sync protocol on eitri, markdown templates on sindri)\n- Same beads.jsonl touched by both machines with divergent bead IDs and lifecycle state\n- `refs/ddx/iterations/` preservation never fired because merges kept succeeding textually while producing semantically incoherent state\n- 12+ \"Merge made by 'ort' strategy\" commits on 2026-04-13 afternoon on eitri alone, each bringing in a sibling local execute-bead worker's output\n\n## Root causes identified\n\n1. No `git fetch origin` at the top of a worker loop iteration — each bead starts from the worker's stale local HEAD (child bead DDX-agent-fetch)\n2. No `git push` on successful landing — successful work stays trapped on the local branch (child bead DDX-agent-push)\n3. Intra-machine sibling workers land via `git merge --no-edit` (ort strategy) instead of rebase, producing a fan of merge commits instead of a linear history (child bead DDX-agent-rebase)\n4. .ddx/beads.jsonl is a git-tracked mutable ledger with no concurrent-write semantics (child bead DDX-agent-tracker)\n5. No divergence monitoring — the 134/336 split grew silently over two weeks (child bead DDX-agent-monitor)\n6. Multi-machine topology undocumented — which machine is authoritative, who pulls, who pushes (child bead DDX-agent-topology)\n\n## Primary source evidence\n\n- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go:59-69 — Merge function uses `git merge --no-edit` with 'ort' strategy, no rebase alternative, commits a 'chore: checkpoint before merge' before attempting each merge\n- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_commit.go — has ZERO references to push/fetch/origin\n- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:~320 — worker loop runs Executor.Execute without any fetch-origin or ancestry-check step\n\n## Impact\n\nAny project that has run the execute-loop for more than a few days across more than one machine is silently divergent from origin. Content written by this session cannot be pushed to the axon origin without a multi-hour manual reconciliation. The axon repo currently has two weeks of unshared work on two machines.\n\n## Scope of this epic\n\nSix child beads covering: fetch-before-work, push-on-success, rebase-vs-merge, tracker concurrency, divergence monitoring, multi-machine topology docs. Each is actionable and self-contained.\n\n## Blocks\n\nAny further `ddx agent execute-loop` runs on projects with more than one participating machine should be paused until at least the first three child beads (fetch, push, rebase) are landed. Running the loop in the current state compounds divergence.","id":"ddx-b7ee0d6f","issue_type":"epic","labels":["bug","execute-loop","orchestration","epic"],"notes":"## Reframed 2026-04-14 — design anchor: ddx-8746d8a6\n\nThe six original child beads (bd42f640, 6aa50e57, 83ec1430, 2791dc4f, 42dd982c, b0374901) identified pieces of the gap correctly but did not compose into a coherent end-to-end design. Per an opus-led architectural review on 2026-04-14, the root problem is not that execute-loop's git operations are missing pieces; it is that the whole model is wrong.\n\nTarget model (from the ddx user): human-PR queue. Workers are contributors, a centralized land coordinator is the maintainer, target-ref writes are serialized through a single writer that always sees the current tip.\n\nThe design for this model is in **ddx-8746d8a6 \"execute-loop: adopt human-PR landing model end-to-end (meta redesign)\"**. Treat ddx-8746d8a6 as the authoritative design anchor for this epic. .ddx/executions/20260423T235117-e92c3b5c/prompt.md:5:## Observed

The Efficacy page at `/nodes/.../projects/.../efficacy` loads very slowly. User feedback: efficacy should really be "an aggregation of sessions grouped by provider / harness / model" — which is not what the current implementation does.

## Ground truth (current implementation)

`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:676-833` —

- `EfficacyRows` builds its rollup from **bead evidence events** (`kind:routing` and `kind:cost` on every closed bead), not from sessions.
- Computation path: `store.ReadAll()` → for every closed bead, read its event file → filter for routing/cost → per-(harness, provider, model) aggregate.
- There is a cache (`efficacyMemo`), keyed by a fingerprint that itself walks every event on every closed bead to compute event count + max timestamp. So cache validation costs almost as much as cache rebuild.
- Cost shape: `O(closed_beads × events_per_bead)` on every request (cache miss) and even on cache hit the fingerprint walk happens.

## Why this is wrong (per user feedback)

- Efficacy is a sessions-view concept: "how is harness/provider/model X doing?" Sessions are the natural row. Bead events are a lossy, indirect proxy — they only exist for *closed* beads, they duplicate session state, and they force coupling between the efficacy feature and bead lifecycle.
- The bead-event path misses: open beads with in-flight sessions, sessions that didn't close a bead, benchmark/quorum sessions, non-execute-bead invocations (e.g., `ddx agent run`).
- The sessions feed is already being reworked into a sharded pointer index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`). Efficacy should read from that index — single source of truth.

## Proposed direction

### Part 1 — Reshape the data source

- Rebuild `EfficacyRows` as an aggregation over `sessions.index` (the sharded index from `ddx-2ceb02fa`), not over bead events.
- Grouping key: `(harness, provider, model)` — optionally filterable by project, bead, label, time range.
- Metrics per row: attempts (count), successes (count + rate), median input/output tokens, median duration, median cost. Identical shape to today; only the source changes.
- Shard awareness: time-range queries map to a shard set (same pattern as `ddx-2ceb02fa`); the default view reads current + previous shard.

### Part 2 — Fixture + baseline (honor user's 1,000+ record floor)

- Perf harness target: aggregate ≥**1,000 session rows** (≥ 10 distinct `(harness, provider, model)` groups) within the budget below.
- Stretch fixture: 50,000 session rows across 24 monthly shards. Measures steady-state behavior as the index grows.
- Baseline captured in the bead notes before any optimization.

### Part 3 — Perf targets (first pass, in-place)

- Default Efficacy view (all-time) on a 10k-session, 2-shard fixture: p95 ≤ 150ms in-process / ≤ 400ms HTTP.
- Date-filtered view (last 30 days, 1 shard): p95 ≤ 60ms in-process / ≤ 200ms HTTP.
- If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, memoize-per-shard, streaming aggregation) **cannot hit these numbers** on the 50k stretch fixture, **do not silently change substrate** — raise the decision with the user. Candidate backends named by the user: axon, sqlite, postgres. Bring a recommendation + rationale, not a fait accompli.

### Part 4 — Drop the bead-event read path

- After the rewrite, remove `efficacyFingerprint`, `buildEfficacySnapshot` event iteration, `attemptsFromEvidence`, and `efficacyMemo`. They become dead weight once the source moves.
- Evidence events on beads remain (they're bead audit trail, not efficacy data); nothing in bead lifecycle changes.

## Out of scope

- The `comparisons` / "compare arms" feature on this page (tracked separately — see companion bead).
- Project-scope filtering UI (the backend support can be added now; the UI widget is a follow-up).
- Cross-machine / federated efficacy rollups.
.ddx/executions/20260423T235117-e92c3b5c/prompt.md:8:**User story:** As a developer reviewing how different harness/provider/model combinations are performing, I expect the Efficacy page to load quickly on a workspace with tens of thousands of sessions, and I expect the rows to reflect every agent invocation — not just those tied to closed beads.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Source change.** `EfficacyRows` resolver reads from the sharded sessions index (`.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, per `ddx-2ceb02fa`). Bead-event read paths (`efficacyFingerprint`, `buildEfficacySnapshot`'s event loop, `attemptsFromEvidence`, `efficacyMemo`) are removed in the same PR — no dead code retained.

2. **Schema parity.** Row shape and field names stay as they are today (rowKey, harness, provider, model, attempts, successes, successRate, medianInputTokens, medianOutputTokens, medianDurationMs, medianCostUsd, warning). UI does not need to change. Optional new filters (`since`, `until`, `projectId`) are added as nullable args with sensible defaults.

3. **Fixture + baseline recorded.** Fixture seeds ≥10,000 session index rows across 2+ monthly shards with ≥10 distinct `(harness, provider, model)` groups. Baseline latencies (before any optimization beyond source change) written to bead notes with hardware + go-version footer.

4. **First-pass perf targets.**
   - All-time rollup on 10k-row, 2-shard fixture: p95 ≤ 150ms in-process / ≤ 400ms HTTP.
   - Last-30-days rollup on same fixture: p95 ≤ 60ms / ≤ 200ms HTTP.
   - Stretch fixture (50k rows × 24 shards): all-time p95 ≤ 400ms in-process / ≤ 1000ms HTTP, or **user is consulted** before choosing a new substrate.

5. **DB-decision protocol (per user directive 2026-04-22).**
   - If targets in (4) cannot be met with in-place optimization (streaming aggregation, per-shard memoization with correct invalidation, selective field read), the PR author posts a short proposal in the bead notes: measured achievable numbers, proposed substrate from {axon, sqlite, postgres}, and migration cost. Implementation of that substrate requires explicit user approval before landing.

6. **Completeness test.** A fixture seeds sessions that would not appear under the old bead-event path (open-bead sessions, `ddx agent run` sessions, benchmark sessions). The new resolver returns rows for each of them. A matching integration test on the old code path (kept as a golden-output comparison for one cycle) asserts the new output is a strict superset in the cases it should be.

7. **Freshness.** A session written via the sessions index writer (from `ddx-2ceb02fa`) is visible in the Efficacy query within 2s. Integration test writes a row and queries the aggregation.

8. **Playwright smoke.** `/efficacy` opens, renders the rollup table, and clicks into `EfficacyAttempts` for one row — all within normal navigation budgets. Uses the 10k-row fixture; asserts the table has ≥5 rows rendered.

9. **Cross-reference.** This bead is a dependent of `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index must exist). If `ddx-2ceb02fa` is not yet landed when this work starts, bead notes record the stub / interim source chosen and the timeline for switching over.

.ddx/executions/20260423T234701-b5579c5c/prompt.md:11:## 2026-04-22 — User directives on fixture size and storage strategy

Two standing instructions from the user that apply to this bead and to all other perf beads (`ddx-ad0db8fd`, `ddx-2ceb02fa`, and any spawned from efficacy/providers work):

1. **Minimum fixture size: 1,000s of records.** The original acceptance criterion (10 projects × 500 beads = 5,000) already meets this for beads, but restate the floor explicitly: **no perf work lands against a fixture smaller than 1,000 records per query shape**. Sessions fixtures, efficacy fixtures, provider/quota history fixtures — all ≥1k rows. Beads fixture stays at 5k; sessions fixture for `ddx-2ceb02fa` should be ≥10k index entries (already specified); efficacy and any new surfaces ≥1k attempts/rows each.

2. **DB backend is on the table.** If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, lightweight index) cannot hit the target latencies on the fixture sizes above, the user is open to introducing a real data store — candidates named: **axon**, **sqlite**, **postgres**. Do not silently pick one: **raise the decision with the user before choosing a path.** Capture the baseline, capture what in-place optimization achieved, then bring the choice (and the rationale) to the user.

These directives supersede any acceptance criterion that implies "optimize in place no matter what." If the numbers say storage needs to change, that's the outcome — but the user decides the substrate.

**AC §8 — Playwright cross-project test routes wrong path**: `beads-smoke.spec.ts:107` navigates to `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/projects/${PROJECT_ID}/beads` for the "cross-project" test, which is the same per-project URL as the 1s test. A cross-project smoke test should hit the all-beads route (e.g., `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/beads` or whatever the actual cross-project URL is) and mock the `beads` query, not `beadsByProject`. The mock for `beads` exists (lines 76-89) but is never exercised because both tests navigate to the project-scoped path.
**AC §9 — Bead notes cross-reference**: The acceptance criteria require this bead's notes to list `ddx-ad0db8fd` and `ddx-2ceb02fa`. The baseline `.md` and code comments mention them, but no `ddx bead update` call or notes update appears in the diff. If the notes were updated via a separate mechanism not captured in this branch, this finding can be dismissed.

## 2026-04-23 — Baseline and perf-thread cross-reference

Baseline recorded in `docs/helix/06-iterate/perf/2026-04-22-baseline.md` and JSON sibling from the 10 projects x 500 beads fixture (5,000 beads total, 1,000 session rows). Environment: `go=go1.26.2 goos=linux goarch=arm64 cpu-cores=12`.

Key p95 results: `bead` 6.72ms in-process / 6.80ms HTTP; `beadsByProject` 8.84ms in-process / 9.29ms HTTP; `beads_crossProject` 62.16ms in-process / 75.80ms HTTP; `beadsByProject_statusOpen` 6.45ms in-process / 6.87ms HTTP.

Cross-reference for inherited harness work: `ddx-ad0db8fd` covers single-bead detail lookup; `ddx-2ceb02fa` covers sessions shard/index work. Both should use/extend this GraphQL perf harness.

Review follow-up: AC §8 cross-project Playwright smoke now navigates to `/nodes/{nodeId}/beads` and exercises the `beads` GraphQL query instead of the project-scoped `beadsByProject` path. Proposed CI posture remains a follow-up +25% p95 regression gate, not implemented in this bead.

## 2026-04-23 — Baseline refresh after session-index fixture alignment

Re-ran `BENCH_REPORT_DATE=2026-04-22 make bench-graphql` after updating the perf fixture to seed current pointer-only session-index shards instead of legacy `sessions.jsonl`. Refreshed artifacts: `docs/helix/06-iterate/perf/2026-04-22-baseline.md` and `.json`.

Refreshed p95 results: `bead` 7.23ms in-process / 7.29ms HTTP; `beadsByProject` 7.18ms in-process / 7.83ms HTTP; `beads_crossProject` 62.49ms in-process / 67.29ms HTTP; `beadsByProject_statusOpen` 6.74ms in-process / 7.73ms HTTP; `agentSessions` 13.45ms in-process / 14.19ms HTTP.

## 2026-04-23 — Final `make bench-graphql` verification

After fixing `cli/Makefile` to pass an absolute `DDX_BENCH_REPORT_DIR`, `BENCH_REPORT_DATE=2026-04-22 make bench-graphql` writes to the intended repo docs path. .ddx/executions/20260423T052916-b268263f/prompt.md:5:## Observed

The Efficacy page at `/nodes/.../projects/.../efficacy` loads very slowly. User feedback: efficacy should really be "an aggregation of sessions grouped by provider / harness / model" — which is not what the current implementation does.

## Ground truth (current implementation)

`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:676-833` —

- `EfficacyRows` builds its rollup from **bead evidence events** (`kind:routing` and `kind:cost` on every closed bead), not from sessions.
- Computation path: `store.ReadAll()` → for every closed bead, read its event file → filter for routing/cost → per-(harness, provider, model) aggregate.
- There is a cache (`efficacyMemo`), keyed by a fingerprint that itself walks every event on every closed bead to compute event count + max timestamp. So cache validation costs almost as much as cache rebuild.
- Cost shape: `O(closed_beads × events_per_bead)` on every request (cache miss) and even on cache hit the fingerprint walk happens.

## Why this is wrong (per user feedback)

- Efficacy is a sessions-view concept: "how is harness/provider/model X doing?" Sessions are the natural row. Bead events are a lossy, indirect proxy — they only exist for *closed* beads, they duplicate session state, and they force coupling between the efficacy feature and bead lifecycle.
- The bead-event path misses: open beads with in-flight sessions, sessions that didn't close a bead, benchmark/quorum sessions, non-execute-bead invocations (e.g., `ddx agent run`).
- The sessions feed is already being reworked into a sharded pointer index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`). Efficacy should read from that index — single source of truth.

## Proposed direction

### Part 1 — Reshape the data source

- Rebuild `EfficacyRows` as an aggregation over `sessions.index` (the sharded index from `ddx-2ceb02fa`), not over bead events.
- Grouping key: `(harness, provider, model)` — optionally filterable by project, bead, label, time range.
- Metrics per row: attempts (count), successes (count + rate), median input/output tokens, median duration, median cost. Identical shape to today; only the source changes.
- Shard awareness: time-range queries map to a shard set (same pattern as `ddx-2ceb02fa`); the default view reads current + previous shard.

### Part 2 — Fixture + baseline (honor user's 1,000+ record floor)

- Perf harness target: aggregate ≥**1,000 session rows** (≥ 10 distinct `(harness, provider, model)` groups) within the budget below.
- Stretch fixture: 50,000 session rows across 24 monthly shards. Measures steady-state behavior as the index grows.
- Baseline captured in the bead notes before any optimization.

### Part 3 — Perf targets (first pass, in-place)

- Default Efficacy view (all-time) on a 10k-session, 2-shard fixture: p95 ≤ 150ms in-process / ≤ 400ms HTTP.
- Date-filtered view (last 30 days, 1 shard): p95 ≤ 60ms in-process / ≤ 200ms HTTP.
- If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, memoize-per-shard, streaming aggregation) **cannot hit these numbers** on the 50k stretch fixture, **do not silently change substrate** — raise the decision with the user. Candidate backends named by the user: axon, sqlite, postgres. Bring a recommendation + rationale, not a fait accompli.

### Part 4 — Drop the bead-event read path

- After the rewrite, remove `efficacyFingerprint`, `buildEfficacySnapshot` event iteration, `attemptsFromEvidence`, and `efficacyMemo`. They become dead weight once the source moves.
- Evidence events on beads remain (they're bead audit trail, not efficacy data); nothing in bead lifecycle changes.

## Out of scope

- The `comparisons` / "compare arms" feature on this page (tracked separately — see companion bead).
- Project-scope filtering UI (the backend support can be added now; the UI widget is a follow-up).
- Cross-machine / federated efficacy rollups.
.ddx/executions/20260423T052916-b268263f/prompt.md:8:**User story:** As a developer reviewing how different harness/provider/model combinations are performing, I expect the Efficacy page to load quickly on a workspace with tens of thousands of sessions, and I expect the rows to reflect every agent invocation — not just those tied to closed beads.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Source change.** `EfficacyRows` resolver reads from the sharded sessions index (`.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, per `ddx-2ceb02fa`). Bead-event read paths (`efficacyFingerprint`, `buildEfficacySnapshot`'s event loop, `attemptsFromEvidence`, `efficacyMemo`) are removed in the same PR — no dead code retained.

2. **Schema parity.** Row shape and field names stay as they are today (rowKey, harness, provider, model, attempts, successes, successRate, medianInputTokens, medianOutputTokens, medianDurationMs, medianCostUsd, warning). UI does not need to change. Optional new filters (`since`, `until`, `projectId`) are added as nullable args with sensible defaults.

3. **Fixture + baseline recorded.** Fixture seeds ≥10,000 session index rows across 2+ monthly shards with ≥10 distinct `(harness, provider, model)` groups. Baseline latencies (before any optimization beyond source change) written to bead notes with hardware + go-version footer.

4. **First-pass perf targets.**
   - All-time rollup on 10k-row, 2-shard fixture: p95 ≤ 150ms in-process / ≤ 400ms HTTP.
   - Last-30-days rollup on same fixture: p95 ≤ 60ms / ≤ 200ms HTTP.
   - Stretch fixture (50k rows × 24 shards): all-time p95 ≤ 400ms in-process / ≤ 1000ms HTTP, or **user is consulted** before choosing a new substrate.

5. **DB-decision protocol (per user directive 2026-04-22).**
   - If targets in (4) cannot be met with in-place optimization (streaming aggregation, per-shard memoization with correct invalidation, selective field read), the PR author posts a short proposal in the bead notes: measured achievable numbers, proposed substrate from {axon, sqlite, postgres}, and migration cost. Implementation of that substrate requires explicit user approval before landing.

6. **Completeness test.** A fixture seeds sessions that would not appear under the old bead-event path (open-bead sessions, `ddx agent run` sessions, benchmark sessions). The new resolver returns rows for each of them. A matching integration test on the old code path (kept as a golden-output comparison for one cycle) asserts the new output is a strict superset in the cases it should be.

7. **Freshness.** A session written via the sessions index writer (from `ddx-2ceb02fa`) is visible in the Efficacy query within 2s. Integration test writes a row and queries the aggregation.

8. **Playwright smoke.** `/efficacy` opens, renders the rollup table, and clicks into `EfficacyAttempts` for one row — all within normal navigation budgets. Uses the 10k-row fixture; asserts the table has ≥5 rows rendered.

9. **Cross-reference.** This bead is a dependent of `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index must exist). If `ddx-2ceb02fa` is not yet landed when this work starts, bead notes record the stub / interim source chosen and the timeline for switching over.

.ddx/executions/20260423T035055-06c99b23/prompt.md:11:## 2026-04-22 — User directives on fixture size and storage strategy

Two standing instructions from the user that apply to this bead and to all other perf beads (`ddx-ad0db8fd`, `ddx-2ceb02fa`, and any spawned from efficacy/providers work):

1. **Minimum fixture size: 1,000s of records.** The original acceptance criterion (10 projects × 500 beads = 5,000) already meets this for beads, but restate the floor explicitly: **no perf work lands against a fixture smaller than 1,000 records per query shape**. Sessions fixtures, efficacy fixtures, provider/quota history fixtures — all ≥1k rows. Beads fixture stays at 5k; sessions fixture for `ddx-2ceb02fa` should be ≥10k index entries (already specified); efficacy and any new surfaces ≥1k attempts/rows each.

2. **DB backend is on the table.** If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, lightweight index) cannot hit the target latencies on the fixture sizes above, the user is open to introducing a real data store — candidates named: **axon**, **sqlite**, **postgres**. Do not silently pick one: **raise the decision with the user before choosing a path.** Capture the baseline, capture what in-place optimization achieved, then bring the choice (and the rationale) to the user.

These directives supersede any acceptance criterion that implies "optimize in place no matter what." If the numbers say storage needs to change, that's the outcome — but the user decides the substrate.

**AC §8 — Playwright cross-project test routes wrong path**: `beads-smoke.spec.ts:107` navigates to `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/projects/${PROJECT_ID}/beads` for the "cross-project" test, which is the same per-project URL as the 1s test. A cross-project smoke test should hit the all-beads route (e.g., `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/beads` or whatever the actual cross-project URL is) and mock the `beads` query, not `beadsByProject`. The mock for `beads` exists (lines 76-89) but is never exercised because both tests navigate to the project-scoped path.

Go tests that exercise the server (or anything that wires up a `ServerState`) call `RegisterProject` with a temp-dir path. The state file they write to is **not isolated** — it defaults to the shared XDG location (`serverAddrDir()` at `cli/internal/server/server.go:67`) unless the test explicitly overrides `XDG_DATA_HOME` or constructs state via the `tc.StateDir` injection path.\n2. There is no test-side deregistration on teardown. Temp dirs get cleaned up by `t.TempDir()`'s own finalizer, but the project entry in the production state file stays.\n3. The reachability sweep (`ServerState.migrate` at `cli/internal/server/state.go:85-148`) tombstones missing-path entries and drops them 24h later. With thousands of entries being added per test run, the rate of addition outpaces the 24h GC.\n\n## Perf amplifier (cross-cutting)\n\nBoth `GetBeadSnapshots` (`state_graphql.go:49-105`) and `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` (`state_graphql.go:476-489`) iterate every registered project per request. This is part of why `ddx-9ce6842a` (beads perf) and `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions feed) feel slow in practice even before their own N·M bugs are considered.\n\n## Proposed direction\n\nThree independently-landable fixes:\n\n### Fix A — Test isolation (highest leverage)\n\n- Audit every test that calls `ServerState` / `RegisterProject` / boots a server. Each one must point at an isolated state dir (`t.TempDir()` + `XDG_DATA_HOME` override, or direct `tc.StateDir` injection).\n- Add a linting test: parse `cli/internal/server/**/*_test.go` for constructions of `ServerState` or HTTP server test helpers and assert each uses an isolated state dir. Fail CI otherwise.\n- This is the real fix. These are never real projects.\n- Unit test: seed a state with mixed real + fake-test-dir entries, run sweep, assert only the real ones remain.\n\n### Fix C — Manual cleanup CLI\n\n- `ddx server state prune` — read state file, apply Fix B's rules, write backup to `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json.bak-\u003ctimestamp\u003e`, write cleaned state, print summary (X dropped, Y kept).\n- Useful for recovery on machines that have already accumulated pollution — like the reporter's. Keep even after Fix A lands.\n- Dry-run flag prints the summary without writing.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Moving state to SQLite.\n- Per-user project management UI. (Follow-up if the projects list becomes a real operator surface.)\n- Renaming \"001\" away from a confusing default. The name field is just `filepath.Base(path)`; fixing Fix A makes this question moot.",

.ddx/executions/20260423T040113-a89a35ab/prompt.md:5:## Observed

`~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json` on a developer machine contains **7,343 registered projects**. Composition:

- **6,936 named "001"** — all are Go test temp dirs like `/tmp/TestAgentCapabilitiesCommandJSON1451829170/001`, `/tmp/TestAgentCheckSuccess3666443068/001`.
- **344 named "002"** — same pattern in tests that use `002/`.
- **~60 real projects** (ddx, helix, axon, plus per-branch worktrees).
- Only 135 of the 6,936 are marked unreachable; the rest are "alive" because their `/tmp` dir still exists or because `LastSeen` is recent.
- State file size: 1.8 MB.

## Root cause

1. Go tests that exercise the server (or anything that wires up a `ServerState`) call `RegisterProject` with a temp-dir path. The state file they write to is **not isolated** — it defaults to the shared XDG location (`serverAddrDir()` at `cli/internal/server/server.go:67`) unless the test explicitly overrides `XDG_DATA_HOME` or constructs state via the `tc.StateDir` injection path.
2. There is no test-side deregistration on teardown. Temp dirs get cleaned up by `t.TempDir()`'s own finalizer, but the project entry in the production state file stays.
3. The reachability sweep (`ServerState.migrate` at `cli/internal/server/state.go:85-148`) tombstones missing-path entries and drops them 24h later. With thousands of entries being added per test run, the rate of addition outpaces the 24h GC.

## Perf amplifier (cross-cutting)

Both `GetBeadSnapshots` (`state_graphql.go:49-105`) and `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` (`state_graphql.go:476-489`) iterate every registered project per request. With N=7343 that's ~7k `os.Stat` + `store.ReadAll` attempts per bead-list or sessions query, mostly against dead paths. This is part of why `ddx-9ce6842a` (beads perf) and `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions feed) feel slow in practice even before their own N·M bugs are considered.

## Proposed direction

Three independently-landable fixes:

### Fix A — Test isolation (highest leverage)

- Audit every test that calls `ServerState` / `RegisterProject` / boots a server. Each one must point at an isolated state dir (`t.TempDir()` + `XDG_DATA_HOME` override, or direct `tc.StateDir` injection).
- Add a linting test: parse `cli/internal/server/**/*_test.go` for constructions of `ServerState` or HTTP server test helpers and assert each uses an isolated state dir. Fail CI otherwise.
- This is the real fix. The rest is damage mitigation.

### Fix B — Smarter sweep (defense in depth)

- Sweep: any path under `/tmp`, `/private/tmp`, `/var/folders/*/T/` (macOS test temp root), or matching `/tmp/Test*` should be treated as a **candidate test-dir project** and dropped immediately on first sweep that finds it missing — no 24h tombstone.
- Sweep should also drop entries where `Path` contains any `Test[A-Z]\w+\d+` segment (the Go testing convention), regardless of whether the dir currently exists. These are never real projects.
- Unit test: seed a state with mixed real + fake-test-dir entries, run sweep, assert only the real ones remain.

### Fix C — Manual cleanup CLI

- `ddx server state prune` — read state file, apply Fix B's rules, write backup to `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json.bak-<timestamp>`, write cleaned state, print summary (X dropped, Y kept).
- Useful for recovery on machines that have already accumulated pollution — like the reporter's. Keep even after Fix A lands.
- Dry-run flag prints the summary without writing.

## Out of scope

- Moving state to SQLite.
- Per-user project management UI. (Follow-up if the projects list becomes a real operator surface.)
- Renaming "001" away from a confusing default. The name field is just `filepath.Base(path)`; fixing Fix A makes this question moot.
.ddx/executions/20260423T040113-a89a35ab/prompt.md:8:**User story:** As a developer running `go test`, my test runs do not leave behind registered projects in my real `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json`. When a machine has already been polluted, I can prune it with one command. The server sweep is aggressive enough that obvious test-dir pollution does not survive a restart.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Fix A — test isolation enforced by CI.**
   - Every existing test that instantiates `ServerState` or boots a server helper is migrated to an isolated state dir. No test writes to `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json`.
   - A lint test (Go or shell) parses `cli/internal/server/**/*_test.go` (and any other directories that wire up `ServerState`) and fails if it finds a construction that does not pass an explicit isolated state path or override `XDG_DATA_HOME` first. Test cases added for both the allowed-pattern and the disallowed-pattern.

2. **Fix B — sweep recognizes test-dir pollution.**
   - `ServerState.migrate` drops entries whose `Path` matches any of:
     - prefix `/tmp/`, `/private/tmp/`, `/var/folders/`
     - regex `/Test[A-Z][A-Za-z0-9_]*\d+/` anywhere in the path
   - Missing paths matching these patterns are dropped on first sweep (no 24h tombstone).
   - Reachable paths matching these patterns are still dropped (they are never real projects — the test dir may exist transiently but is never the user's registered project).
   - Unit test seeds a fixture state with one real project, one `/tmp/TestFoo123/001` missing, one `/tmp/TestBar456/002` still on disk, and one `/private/tmp/TestMac789/001`; asserts only the real project survives.

3. **Fix C — pruning CLI.**
   - `ddx server state prune [--dry-run] [--state <path>]` applies the Fix B rules to an on-disk state file.
   - Writes `server-state.json.bak-<YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS>` before overwriting.
   - Prints summary: `Pruned X of Y entries (Z kept)`. Dry-run prints the summary without writing.
   - Integration test uses a fixture state containing 10 real + 100 fake entries, runs the command, asserts 10 remain and a backup file with exactly 110 entries was written.

4. **Perf amplifier — regression test.**
   - A test asserts `GetBeadSnapshots` does **not** call `os.Stat` on any project already tombstoned or pattern-filtered. (Implementation detail: sweep on startup + skip tombstoned in the hot loop.)
   - If Fix A+B are in place, a fresh `go test ./...` run followed by a server restart produces a state file with ≤ 20 projects on a typical dev machine. Asserted via a CI job that runs the tests, starts the server briefly, inspects the state file, and fails if test-dir patterns are present.

5. **Backward compatibility.**
   - The prune CLI is safe to run on an existing polluted state file. The sweep upgrade is safe on restart — no data loss for real projects.
   - On upgrade, a note is logged: "Pruned X phantom test-dir projects from state file."

6. **Cross-reference.**
   - Bead notes link to `ddx-9ce6842a` (beads perf) and `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions feed) as the two surfaces whose perf problems are amplified by this bug. Those beads' fixture sizing assumptions were correct for a clean state file; the operator experience will still feel slow on a polluted machine until Fix C is run.

.ddx/executions/20260423T041824-57af88cc/prompt.md:5:## Observed

The Efficacy page at `/nodes/.../projects/.../efficacy` loads very slowly. User feedback: efficacy should really be "an aggregation of sessions grouped by provider / harness / model" — which is not what the current implementation does.

## Ground truth (current implementation)

`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:676-833` —

- `EfficacyRows` builds its rollup from **bead evidence events** (`kind:routing` and `kind:cost` on every closed bead), not from sessions.
- Computation path: `store.ReadAll()` → for every closed bead, read its event file → filter for routing/cost → per-(harness, provider, model) aggregate.
- There is a cache (`efficacyMemo`), keyed by a fingerprint that itself walks every event on every closed bead to compute event count + max timestamp. So cache validation costs almost as much as cache rebuild.
- Cost shape: `O(closed_beads × events_per_bead)` on every request (cache miss) and even on cache hit the fingerprint walk happens.

## Why this is wrong (per user feedback)

- Efficacy is a sessions-view concept: "how is harness/provider/model X doing?" Sessions are the natural row. Bead events are a lossy, indirect proxy — they only exist for *closed* beads, they duplicate session state, and they force coupling between the efficacy feature and bead lifecycle.
- The bead-event path misses: open beads with in-flight sessions, sessions that didn't close a bead, benchmark/quorum sessions, non-execute-bead invocations (e.g., `ddx agent run`).
- The sessions feed is already being reworked into a sharded pointer index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`). Efficacy should read from that index — single source of truth.

## Proposed direction

### Part 1 — Reshape the data source

- Rebuild `EfficacyRows` as an aggregation over `sessions.index` (the sharded index from `ddx-2ceb02fa`), not over bead events.
- Grouping key: `(harness, provider, model)` — optionally filterable by project, bead, label, time range.
- Metrics per row: attempts (count), successes (count + rate), median input/output tokens, median duration, median cost. Identical shape to today; only the source changes.
- Shard awareness: time-range queries map to a shard set (same pattern as `ddx-2ceb02fa`); the default view reads current + previous shard.

### Part 2 — Fixture + baseline (honor user's 1,000+ record floor)

- Perf harness target: aggregate ≥**1,000 session rows** (≥ 10 distinct `(harness, provider, model)` groups) within the budget below.
- Stretch fixture: 50,000 session rows across 24 monthly shards. Measures steady-state behavior as the index grows.
- Baseline captured in the bead notes before any optimization.

### Part 3 — Perf targets (first pass, in-place)

- Default Efficacy view (all-time) on a 10k-session, 2-shard fixture: p95 ≤ 150ms in-process / ≤ 400ms HTTP.
- Date-filtered view (last 30 days, 1 shard): p95 ≤ 60ms in-process / ≤ 200ms HTTP.
- If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, memoize-per-shard, streaming aggregation) **cannot hit these numbers** on the 50k stretch fixture, **do not silently change substrate** — raise the decision with the user. Candidate backends named by the user: axon, sqlite, postgres. Bring a recommendation + rationale, not a fait accompli.

### Part 4 — Drop the bead-event read path

- After the rewrite, remove `efficacyFingerprint`, `buildEfficacySnapshot` event iteration, `attemptsFromEvidence`, and `efficacyMemo`. They become dead weight once the source moves.
- Evidence events on beads remain (they're bead audit trail, not efficacy data); nothing in bead lifecycle changes.

## Out of scope

- The `comparisons` / "compare arms" feature on this page (tracked separately — see companion bead).
- Project-scope filtering UI (the backend support can be added now; the UI widget is a follow-up).
- Cross-machine / federated efficacy rollups.
.ddx/executions/20260423T041824-57af88cc/prompt.md:8:**User story:** As a developer reviewing how different harness/provider/model combinations are performing, I expect the Efficacy page to load quickly on a workspace with tens of thousands of sessions, and I expect the rows to reflect every agent invocation — not just those tied to closed beads.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Source change.** `EfficacyRows` resolver reads from the sharded sessions index (`.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, per `ddx-2ceb02fa`). Bead-event read paths (`efficacyFingerprint`, `buildEfficacySnapshot`'s event loop, `attemptsFromEvidence`, `efficacyMemo`) are removed in the same PR — no dead code retained.

2. **Schema parity.** Row shape and field names stay as they are today (rowKey, harness, provider, model, attempts, successes, successRate, medianInputTokens, medianOutputTokens, medianDurationMs, medianCostUsd, warning). UI does not need to change. Optional new filters (`since`, `until`, `projectId`) are added as nullable args with sensible defaults.

3. **Fixture + baseline recorded.** Fixture seeds ≥10,000 session index rows across 2+ monthly shards with ≥10 distinct `(harness, provider, model)` groups. Baseline latencies (before any optimization beyond source change) written to bead notes with hardware + go-version footer.

4. **First-pass perf targets.**
   - All-time rollup on 10k-row, 2-shard fixture: p95 ≤ 150ms in-process / ≤ 400ms HTTP.
   - Last-30-days rollup on same fixture: p95 ≤ 60ms / ≤ 200ms HTTP.
   - Stretch fixture (50k rows × 24 shards): all-time p95 ≤ 400ms in-process / ≤ 1000ms HTTP, or **user is consulted** before choosing a new substrate.

5. **DB-decision protocol (per user directive 2026-04-22).**
   - If targets in (4) cannot be met with in-place optimization (streaming aggregation, per-shard memoization with correct invalidation, selective field read), the PR author posts a short proposal in the bead notes: measured achievable numbers, proposed substrate from {axon, sqlite, postgres}, and migration cost. Implementation of that substrate requires explicit user approval before landing.

6. **Completeness test.** A fixture seeds sessions that would not appear under the old bead-event path (open-bead sessions, `ddx agent run` sessions, benchmark sessions). The new resolver returns rows for each of them. A matching integration test on the old code path (kept as a golden-output comparison for one cycle) asserts the new output is a strict superset in the cases it should be.

7. **Freshness.** A session written via the sessions index writer (from `ddx-2ceb02fa`) is visible in the Efficacy query within 2s. Integration test writes a row and queries the aggregation.

8. **Playwright smoke.** `/efficacy` opens, renders the rollup table, and clicks into `EfficacyAttempts` for one row — all within normal navigation budgets. Uses the 10k-row fixture; asserts the table has ≥5 rows rendered.

9. **Cross-reference.** This bead is a dependent of `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index must exist). If `ddx-2ceb02fa` is not yet landed when this work starts, bead notes record the stub / interim source chosen and the timeline for switching over.
.ddx/executions/20260423T041824-57af88cc/prompt.md:11:Efficacy now depends on ddx-2ceb02fa. The current worktree contains the sharded sessions index implementation at .ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl, so this pass reads that substrate directly; no interim source or stub was used.

Source-change baseline before per-shard memoization: fixture seeds 10,000 session index rows across 2 monthly shards with 10 distinct harness/provider/model groups, plus stretch fixture of 50,000 rows across 24 monthly shards. Measured with go test ./internal/server/graphql -run TestEfficacyRowsDateFilterAndPerfTargets -count=1 -v.

Source-only results:
- 10k all-time in-process p95: 46.87735ms (target <=150ms)
- 10k all-time HTTP p95: 47.756933ms (target <=400ms)
- 10k last-30-days in-process p95: 24.356549ms (target <=60ms)
- 10k last-30-days HTTP p95: 25.628964ms (target <=200ms)
- 50k x 24-shard all-time in-process p95: 279.266233ms (target <=400ms)
- 50k x 24-shard all-time HTTP p95: 300.428036ms (target <=1000ms)

Final implementation adds shard-stat invalidated session-index memoization. Final measured results:
- 10k all-time in-process p95: 1.368873ms
- 10k all-time HTTP p95: 3.202329ms
- 10k last-30-days in-process p95: 1.742706ms
- 10k last-30-days HTTP p95: 2.049998ms
- 50k x 24-shard all-time in-process p95: 242.886602ms
- 50k x 24-shard all-time HTTP p95: 886.748µs

DB-decision protocol: in-place session-index aggregation met the first-pass and stretch targets, so no axon/sqlite/postgres substrate proposal is required in this pass. Race-detector runs skip the perf-threshold test because race instrumentation invalidates latency targets; non-race CI enforces them.

That is only 12 pre-aggregated mocked rows, not a seeded 10k session-index fixture, so it does not validate page behavior or navigation budget against the real backend fixture required by AC8.

.ddx/executions/20260423T005714-24085f34/prompt.md:11:## 2026-04-22 — User directives on fixture size and storage strategy

Two standing instructions from the user that apply to this bead and to all other perf beads (`ddx-ad0db8fd`, `ddx-2ceb02fa`, and any spawned from efficacy/providers work):

1. **Minimum fixture size: 1,000s of records.** The original acceptance criterion (10 projects × 500 beads = 5,000) already meets this for beads, but restate the floor explicitly: **no perf work lands against a fixture smaller than 1,000 records per query shape**. Sessions fixtures, efficacy fixtures, provider/quota history fixtures — all ≥1k rows. Beads fixture stays at 5k; sessions fixture for `ddx-2ceb02fa` should be ≥10k index entries (already specified); efficacy and any new surfaces ≥1k attempts/rows each.

2. **DB backend is on the table.** If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, lightweight index) cannot hit the target latencies on the fixture sizes above, the user is open to introducing a real data store — candidates named: **axon**, **sqlite**, **postgres**. Do not silently pick one: **raise the decision with the user before choosing a path.** Capture the baseline, capture what in-place optimization achieved, then bring the choice (and the rationale) to the user.

These directives supersede any acceptance criterion that implies "optimize in place no matter what." If the numbers say storage needs to change, that's the outcome — but the user decides the substrate.

**AC §8 — Playwright cross-project test routes wrong path**: `beads-smoke.spec.ts:107` navigates to `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/projects/${PROJECT_ID}/beads` for the "cross-project" test, which is the same per-project URL as the 1s test. A cross-project smoke test should hit the all-beads route (e.g., `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/beads` or whatever the actual cross-project URL is) and mock the `beads` query, not `beadsByProject`. The mock for `beads` exists (lines 76-89) but is never exercised because both tests navigate to the project-scoped path.
**AC §9 — Bead notes cross-reference**: The acceptance criteria require this bead's notes to list `ddx-ad0db8fd` and `ddx-2ceb02fa`. The baseline `.md` and code comments mention them, but no `ddx bead update` call or notes update appears in the diff. If the notes were updated via a separate mechanism not captured in this branch, this finding can be dismissed.
.ddx/executions/20260423T010616-b2d3b939/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Observed\n\n`~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json` on a developer machine contains **7,343 registered projects**. .ddx/executions/20260423T010616-b2d3b939/prompt.md:5:## Observed

`~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json` on a developer machine contains **7,343 registered projects**. Composition:

- **6,936 named "001"** — all are Go test temp dirs like `/tmp/TestAgentCapabilitiesCommandJSON1451829170/001`, `/tmp/TestAgentCheckSuccess3666443068/001`.
- **344 named "002"** — same pattern in tests that use `002/`.
- **~60 real projects** (ddx, helix, axon, plus per-branch worktrees).
- Only 135 of the 6,936 are marked unreachable; the rest are "alive" because their `/tmp` dir still exists or because `LastSeen` is recent.
- State file size: 1.8 MB.

## Root cause

1. Go tests that exercise the server (or anything that wires up a `ServerState`) call `RegisterProject` with a temp-dir path. The state file they write to is **not isolated** — it defaults to the shared XDG location (`serverAddrDir()` at `cli/internal/server/server.go:67`) unless the test explicitly overrides `XDG_DATA_HOME` or constructs state via the `tc.StateDir` injection path.
2. There is no test-side deregistration on teardown. Temp dirs get cleaned up by `t.TempDir()`'s own finalizer, but the project entry in the production state file stays.
3. The reachability sweep (`ServerState.migrate` at `cli/internal/server/state.go:85-148`) tombstones missing-path entries and drops them 24h later. With thousands of entries being added per test run, the rate of addition outpaces the 24h GC.

## Perf amplifier (cross-cutting)

Both `GetBeadSnapshots` (`state_graphql.go:49-105`) and `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` (`state_graphql.go:476-489`) iterate every registered project per request. With N=7343 that's ~7k `os.Stat` + `store.ReadAll` attempts per bead-list or sessions query, mostly against dead paths. This is part of why `ddx-9ce6842a` (beads perf) and `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions feed) feel slow in practice even before their own N·M bugs are considered.

## Proposed direction

Three independently-landable fixes:

### Fix A — Test isolation (highest leverage)

- Audit every test that calls `ServerState` / `RegisterProject` / boots a server. Each one must point at an isolated state dir (`t.TempDir()` + `XDG_DATA_HOME` override, or direct `tc.StateDir` injection).
- Add a linting test: parse `cli/internal/server/**/*_test.go` for constructions of `ServerState` or HTTP server test helpers and assert each uses an isolated state dir. Fail CI otherwise.
- This is the real fix. The rest is damage mitigation.

### Fix B — Smarter sweep (defense in depth)

- Sweep: any path under `/tmp`, `/private/tmp`, `/var/folders/*/T/` (macOS test temp root), or matching `/tmp/Test*` should be treated as a **candidate test-dir project** and dropped immediately on first sweep that finds it missing — no 24h tombstone.
- Sweep should also drop entries where `Path` contains any `Test[A-Z]\w+\d+` segment (the Go testing convention), regardless of whether the dir currently exists. These are never real projects.
- Unit test: seed a state with mixed real + fake-test-dir entries, run sweep, assert only the real ones remain.

### Fix C — Manual cleanup CLI

- `ddx server state prune` — read state file, apply Fix B's rules, write backup to `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json.bak-<timestamp>`, write cleaned state, print summary (X dropped, Y kept).
- Useful for recovery on machines that have already accumulated pollution — like the reporter's. Keep even after Fix A lands.
- Dry-run flag prints the summary without writing.

## Out of scope

- Moving state to SQLite.
- Per-user project management UI. (Follow-up if the projects list becomes a real operator surface.)
- Renaming "001" away from a confusing default. The name field is just `filepath.Base(path)`; fixing Fix A makes this question moot.
.ddx/executions/20260423T010616-b2d3b939/prompt.md:8:**User story:** As a developer running `go test`, my test runs do not leave behind registered projects in my real `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json`. When a machine has already been polluted, I can prune it with one command. The server sweep is aggressive enough that obvious test-dir pollution does not survive a restart.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Fix A — test isolation enforced by CI.**
   - Every existing test that instantiates `ServerState` or boots a server helper is migrated to an isolated state dir. No test writes to `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json`.
   - A lint test (Go or shell) parses `cli/internal/server/**/*_test.go` (and any other directories that wire up `ServerState`) and fails if it finds a construction that does not pass an explicit isolated state path or override `XDG_DATA_HOME` first. Test cases added for both the allowed-pattern and the disallowed-pattern.

2. **Fix B — sweep recognizes test-dir pollution.**
   - `ServerState.migrate` drops entries whose `Path` matches any of:
     - prefix `/tmp/`, `/private/tmp/`, `/var/folders/`
     - regex `/Test[A-Z][A-Za-z0-9_]*\d+/` anywhere in the path
   - Missing paths matching these patterns are dropped on first sweep (no 24h tombstone).
   - Reachable paths matching these patterns are still dropped (they are never real projects — the test dir may exist transiently but is never the user's registered project).
   - Unit test seeds a fixture state with one real project, one `/tmp/TestFoo123/001` missing, one `/tmp/TestBar456/002` still on disk, and one `/private/tmp/TestMac789/001`; asserts only the real project survives.

3. **Fix C — pruning CLI.**
   - `ddx server state prune [--dry-run] [--state <path>]` applies the Fix B rules to an on-disk state file.
   - Writes `server-state.json.bak-<YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS>` before overwriting.
   - Prints summary: `Pruned X of Y entries (Z kept)`. Dry-run prints the summary without writing.
   - Integration test uses a fixture state containing 10 real + 100 fake entries, runs the command, asserts 10 remain and a backup file with exactly 110 entries was written.

4. **Perf amplifier — regression test.**
   - A test asserts `GetBeadSnapshots` does **not** call `os.Stat` on any project already tombstoned or pattern-filtered. (Implementation detail: sweep on startup + skip tombstoned in the hot loop.)
   - If Fix A+B are in place, a fresh `go test ./...` run followed by a server restart produces a state file with ≤ 20 projects on a typical dev machine. Asserted via a CI job that runs the tests, starts the server briefly, inspects the state file, and fails if test-dir patterns are present.

5. **Backward compatibility.**
   - The prune CLI is safe to run on an existing polluted state file. The sweep upgrade is safe on restart — no data loss for real projects.
   - On upgrade, a note is logged: "Pruned X phantom test-dir projects from state file."

6. **Cross-reference.**
   - Bead notes link to `ddx-9ce6842a` (beads perf) and `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions feed) as the two surfaces whose perf problems are amplified by this bug. Those beads' fixture sizing assumptions were correct for a clean state file; the operator experience will still feel slow on a polluted machine until Fix C is run.
